epigraph.text:
Well done, good and faithful servant! You have been 
faithful with a few things; I will put you in charge of many things.

content.text:
When you deposit money in a bank or invest in a bond, you earn interest—a 
fee paid to you for allowing others to use your money. There are two 
fundamental types of interest:

content.title: Simple Interest
content.content:
**Simple interest** is calculated only on the 
original principal:
\[
 A = P(1 + rt)
\]
where $P$ = principal, $r$ = annual interest rate, $t$ = time in years, 
and $A$ = final amount.

content.text:
With simple interest, you earn the same dollar amount each year. But with 
**compound interest**, you earn interest on your interest.

content.title: Compound Interest
content.content:
**Compound interest** is calculated on the 
principal plus accumulated interest. If interest is compounded $n$ times 
per year:
\[
 A = P\left(1 + \frac{r}{n}\right)^{nt}
\]
where $P$ = principal, $r$ = annual interest rate (as a decimal), 
$n$ = compounding periods per year, $t$ = time in years.

content.title: Simple vs. compound interest
content.problem:
Compare simple and compound interest on \$1000 at 5\% for 10 years, with 
annual compounding.
content.solution:
**Simple interest:**
\[
 A = 1000(1 + 0.05 \times 10) = 1000(1.5) = \$1500
\]

**Compound interest:**
\[
 A = 1000(1 + 0.05)^{10} = 1000(1.05)^{10} \approx 1000(1.6289) = \$1628.89
\]

Compound interest yields \$128.89 more than simple interest.

content.text: The more frequently interest is compounded, the more you earn.

content.title: Comparing compounding frequencies
content.problem:
\$5000 is invested at 8\% annual interest for 5 years. Compare the final 
amounts with different compounding frequencies.
content.solution:
Using $A = P\left(1 + \frac{r}{n}\right)^{nt}$ with $P = 5000$, $r = 0.08$, $t = 5$:

| Compounding | $n$ | Calculation | Amount |
| Annually | 1 | $5000(1.08)^5$ | \$7346.64 \\
Semiannually & 2 & $5000(1.04)^{10}$ & \$7401.22 |
| Quarterly | 4 | $5000(1.02)^{20}$ | \$7429.74 \\
Monthly & 12 & $5000(1.006)^{60}$ & \$7449.23 |
| Daily | 365 | $5000(1 + 0.08/365)^{1825}$ | \$7458.80 |

More frequent compounding yields higher returns, but with diminishing 
additional benefit.

content.title: Stewardship and Growth
content.content:
In the Parable of the Talents (Matthew 25:14–30), Jesus praises servants 
who invest and grow what they're given, while condemning the one who 
buried his talent. The master expected his money to at least earn interest: 
"You should have put my money on deposit with the bankers, so that when 
I returned I would have received it back with interest" (v. 27).

Compound interest rewards the faithful steward. Small, consistent 
contributions grow exponentially over time. The mathematics teaches what 
Scripture affirms: diligence and patience in small things lead to 
abundance.

content.text: What happens if we compound infinitely often—continuously?

content.title: Continuous Compounding
content.content:
When interest is compounded **continuously**:
\[
 A = Pe^{rt}
\]
where $P$ = principal, $r$ = annual rate (as decimal), $t$ = time in years, 
and $e \approx 2.71828$.

content.text:
This formula comes from the limit:
\[
 \lim_{n \to \infty} P\left(1 + \frac{r}{n}\right)^{nt} = Pe^{rt}
\]

content.title: Continuous compounding
content.problem:
\$5000 is invested at 8\% compounded continuously for 5 years. Find the 
final amount.
content.solution:
\[
 A = 5000 \cdot e^{0.08 \times 5} = 5000 \cdot e^{0.4} \approx 5000 \times 1.4918 = \$7459.12
\]

Compare this to daily compounding (\$7458.80)—continuous compounding 
gives slightly more, but the difference is small.

content.text:
Sometimes we need to find how long it takes to reach a goal, or what 
interest rate is required.

content.title: Finding the time to double
content.problem: How long does it take for an investment to double at 6\% compounded annually?
content.solution:
We need $A = 2P$:
\[
 2P = P(1.06)^t
\]
\[
 2 = (1.06)^t
\]

We'll solve this using logarithms (covered in the next chapter), but we 
can estimate by trial:
$$
 (1.06)^{10} &\approx 1.79 \text{ (not enough)} \\
 (1.06)^{12} &\approx 2.01 \text{ (just over)}
$$

It takes approximately **12 years** to double at 6\%.

content.title: The Rule of 72
content.content:
To estimate the doubling time for an investment, divide 72 by the interest 
rate (as a percentage):
\[
 \text{Doubling time} \approx \frac{72}{r\%}
\]
At 6\%: $72/6 = 12$ years. At 8\%: $72/8 = 9$ years.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Using the Rule of 72
content.problem:
Estimate how long it takes for:

1. \$1000 to double at 9\% interest

2. An investment to quadruple at 6\% interest
content.solution:
1. Doubling time $\approx 72/9 = 8$ years

2. To quadruple is to double twice. At 6\%, each doubling takes 
 $72/6 = 12$ years, so quadrupling takes approximately **24 years**.

content.text: Sometimes we want to find how much to invest now to reach a future goal.

content.title: Present Value
content.content:
The **present value** is the principal $P$ 
needed now to achieve a future amount $A$:
\[
 P = \frac{A}{\left(1 + \frac{r}{n}\right)^{nt}} \quad \text{or} \quad P = Ae^{-rt}
\]

content.title: Finding present value
content.problem:
How much should you invest now at 5\% compounded monthly to have \$10,000 
in 8 years?
content.solution:
\[
 P = \frac{10000}{\left(1 + \frac{0.05}{12}\right)^{96}} = \frac{10000}{(1.00417)^{96}} \approx \frac{10000}{1.4898} \approx \$6712.10
\]

You need to invest approximately \$6712.10 now.

content.text:
Different compounding frequencies make it hard to compare investments. The 
**Annual Percentage Yield** (APY) or **Effective Annual Rate** 
provides a standard comparison.

content.title: Annual Percentage Yield
content.content:
The **APY** is the equivalent simple 
interest rate that would yield the same return:
\[
 \text{APY} = \left(1 + \frac{r}{n}\right)^n - 1
\]
For continuous compounding: $\text{APY} = e^r - 1$

content.title: Comparing APY
content.problem: Which is better: 5.9\% compounded monthly or 6\% compounded annually?
content.solution:
**5.9\% compounded monthly:**
\[
 \text{APY} = \left(1 + \frac{0.059}{12}\right)^{12} - 1 = (1.00492)^{12} - 1 \approx 0.0607 = 6.07\%
\]

**6\% compounded annually:**
\[
 \text{APY} = (1.06)^1 - 1 = 0.06 = 6.00\%
\]

The 5.9\% rate with monthly compounding is slightly better (APY of 6.07\% 
vs. 6.00\%).

content.title: College savings
content.problem:
Parents deposit \$200 per month into an account earning 7\% compounded 
monthly, starting when their child is born. How much will be available 
when the child turns 18?
content.solution:
This requires the **future value of an annuity** formula:
\[
 A = PMT \cdot \frac{(1 + i)^n - 1}{i}
\]
where $PMT = 200$, $i = 0.07/12 \approx 0.00583$, $n = 18 \times 12 = 216$:
\[
 A = 200 \cdot \frac{(1.00583)^{216} - 1}{0.00583} \approx 200 \cdot \frac{3.507 - 1}{0.00583} \approx 200 \cdot 429.9 \approx \$85{,}980
\]

The family will have approximately \$86,000 for college.

content.title: Generational wealth
content.problem:
A family establishes a trust fund with \$10,000 at 6\% compounded annually, 
to be untouched for 100 years. What will it be worth?
content.solution:
\[
 A = 10000(1.06)^{100} \approx 10000 \times 339.3 = \$3{,}393{,}000
\]

This illustrates the power of patient, multi-generational thinking—a 
modest sum becomes millions. Proverbs 13:22 says, "A good person leaves 
an inheritance for their children's children."

content.title: Credit card debt
content.problem:
A credit card has an 18\% annual rate compounded monthly. If you owe 
\$3000 and make no payments, how much will you owe after 2 years?
content.solution:
\[
 A = 3000\left(1 + \frac{0.18}{12}\right)^{24} = 3000(1.015)^{24} \approx 3000 \times 1.4295 = \$4288.50
\]

The debt grows to over \$4200—an increase of nearly 43\%! This shows why 
paying off high-interest debt quickly is so important.

marginNotes.text:
The plans of the diligent lead to profit 
as surely as haste leads to poverty.
